request_debug.prompt_preview:
You rerank dead-startup candidates for a slopmortem pipeline. Given a fresh pitch and K candidates retrieved by hybrid search, score each candidate from four perspectives, then return ONLY the top 5 ranked from most to least relevant.

Perspectives (each scored 0-10 with a one-sentence rationale):
- business_model: how similar are the revenue, pricing, and customer-relationship shapes?
- market: how similar are the target buyers, market size, and demand drivers?
- gtm: how similar are the go-to-

response.text: {"ranked":[{"candidate_id":"parse::devtools","perspective_scores":{"business_model":{"score":6,"rationale":"Parse offered a hosted backend-as-a-service with cloud infrastructure managed by the vendor, which shares the hosted-control-plane SaaS model but targeted mobile app backends rather than ephemeral preview environments."},"market":{"score":7,"rationale":"Parse targeted developers building apps on cloud infrastructure, a closely adjacent developer audience, though focused on mobile app developers rather than DevOps/platform engineering teams managing preview environments."},"gtm":{"score":6,"rationale":"Both rely on developer-led adoption with self-service onboarding and CLI/SDK tooling, though Parse was acquired and distributed differently post-Facebook acquisition."},"stage_scale":{"score":6,"rationale":"Parse was a well-funded, high-growth developer-tools SaaS that reached significant scale before shutdown, a plausible analog for stage/scale comparison."}},"rationale":"Parse is the closest match as a developer-focused hosted-control-plane SaaS with CLI tooling and a cloud-managed service model, serving a similar developer audience through self-service channels, despite the mobile-backend vs. preview-environment distinction."},{"candidate_id":"rethinkdb::devtools","perspective_scores":{"business_model":{"score":4,"rationale":"RethinkDB was open-source infrastructure software that failed to build a commercial SaaS model, unlike the pitch's subscription-based hosted control plane."},"market":{"score":6,"rationale":"RethinkDB targeted developers and engineering teams building data-intensive applications, overlapping with the devtools/developer-infrastructure buyer persona."},"gtm":{"score":5,"rationale":"Both relied on developer community adoption and open-source distribution, though RethinkDB never successfully layered a paid hosted offering on top."},"stage_scale":{"score":5,"rationale":"RethinkDB was a small, VC-backed devtools company that failed before reaching meaningful commercial scale, a rough analog for an early-stage devtools SaaS failure."}},"rationale":"RethinkDB shares the devtools sector and developer audience but diverges significantly on business model—it was open-source infrastructure without a successful subscription SaaS layer, making it a partial but imperfect match."},{"candidate_id":"knewton::edtech","perspective_scores":{"business_model":{"score":3,"rationale":"Knewton was a B2B SaaS platform with enterprise partnerships and a subscription-style model, loosely analogous in revenue shape but in an entirely different sector and customer type."},"market":{"score":1,"rationale":"Knewton targeted educational publishers and students in STEM, a completely different buyer and demand driver from developer teams managing cloud preview environments."},"gtm":{"score":2,"rationale":"Knewton relied on large publisher partnerships rather than developer-led self-service or CLI-driven adoption."},"stage_scale":{"score":4,"rationale":"Knewton raised over $150 million and was acquired for far less, representing a late-stage scale-up failure somewhat analogous to a well-funded SaaS collapse."}},"rationale":"Knewton shares a B2B SaaS subscription model shape but diverges almost entirely on market, customer type, and GTM motion; included only for business-model structural similarity in absence of better candidates."},{"candidate_id":"altschool::edtech","perspective_scores":{"business_model":{"score":2,"rationale":"AltSchool operated physical schools and pivoted to software, bearing little resemblance to a CLI-plus-hosted-control-plane SaaS subscription model."},"market":{"score":1,"rationale":"AltSchool targeted K-12 students and parents, entirely unrelated to developer teams managing cloud infrastructure."},"gtm":{"score":1,"rationale":"AltSchool used physical school operations and direct-to-institution sales, nothing like developer-led CLI adoption or cloud SaaS distribution."},"stage_scale":{"score":3,"rationale":"AltSchool raised $133 million before pivoting/failing, representing a venture-scale startup collapse, loosely comparable in funding trajectory."}},"rationale":"AltSchool is a weak match with minimal overlap in business model, market, or GTM; included only because the remaining candidates are even less relevant to developer infrastructure SaaS."},{"candidate_id":"pebble::hardware","perspective_scores":{"business_model":{"score":2,"rationale":"Pebble was a hardware and consumer electronics company with a companion app ecosystem, structurally very different from a B2B SaaS subscription control plane."},"market":{"score":2,"rationale":"Pebble targeted consumer smartwatch buyers and developers building watch apps, only tangentially related to the cloud DevOps/platform engineering audience."},"gtm":{"score":3,"rationale":"Pebble did have a developer ecosystem and app platform component, offering a partial analog to developer-community-driven adoption motions."},"stage_scale":{"score":4,"rationale":"Pebble was a VC-backed startup that reached meaningful scale before collapsing, comparable in stage to an early-growth devtools SaaS."}},"rationale":"Pebble has a developer ecosystem component and a companion platform angle that gives it marginal relevance, but as a consumer hardware company it diverges substantially from a B2B developer SaaS for cloud preview environments."}]}
